```python
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def earliestAndLatest(self, n: int, firstPlayer: int, secondPlayer: int) -> List[int]:
        earliest, latest = float('inf'), 0
        memo = {}
        
        def dfs(players, round_num):
            nonlocal earliest, latest
            if tuple(sorted(players)) in memo:
                return memo[tuple(sorted(players))]
            if firstPlayer in players and secondPlayer in players and len(players) > 2:
                earliest = min(earliest, round_num)
                latest = max(latest, round_num)
            if len(players) <= 2:
                return
            winners = []
            for i in range(len(players) // 2):
                winners.append(players[i])
            if len(players) % 2 == 1:
                winners.append(players[len(players) // 2])
            memo[tuple(sorted(players))] = True
            dfs(winners, round_num + 1)
            memo[tuple(sorted(players))] = False
            for i in range(len(players) // 2):
                winners[i] = players[-i - 1]
            dfs(winners, round_num + 1)
        
        dfs(list(range(1, n + 1)), 1)
        return [earliest, latest]
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O(2^n)
space complexity: O(2^n)
energy implications: MEDIUM
explanation: The solution uses depth-first search to simulate all possible tournament outcomes, keeping track of the earliest and latest rounds in which the two best players compete against each other.
